story:
People say “meet your maker” when they mean die. I’d met mine and drunk tea in his kitchen. Howard wasn’t a mad scientist in a hidden lab. He was a weary, kind-eyed man in a small, one-story house, more weight on his shoulders than years could name. He greeted me—“Come on in”—and set a kettle to boil.

I set down my backpack and slid out my prize—the Helix Award of Scientific Excellence. Twenty-three, at least on paper, and I’d done it. The certificate said John Carter, 2025. I hadn’t been Jonah in years. To most people I was John. John Carter. Clone of Jonah Carter, thirty-three. Genetically identical and, everyone said, the better version.

Howard had made me from Jonah’s skin cells, slipped his DNA into a donor egg, grown me in a lab, delivered me with his own hands. Then he was fired. Still, he raised us both. We had the same ringlets, same blue eyes, same early lisp, same love of science. We were mirrors—only one mirror got more light. I charmed teachers, aced tests. Jonah saw a replacement, a threat. “You’re a clone,” he spat. “Spare parts.” I smiled and offered mine. He got grounded. I got pats on the head.

When I was nine, Jonah left. “Bye, John,” he said, like the name burned. At the door he leaned in. “You’re not human. You’re engineered. Dad said you were the first trial for ‘The Future of Humanity.’” He was right. Howard confessed: I was meant to be a template—polite, peaceful, smart. If everyone was like me, no wars. I told him it wasn’t right. People aren’t prototypes. He hugged me and said I could be my own person. I also worried I’d live less long than Jonah—he was ten when my DNA was taken; in my head, that shaved ten years off me. Maybe that’s not how biology works, but fear rarely reads journals.

Back in the kitchen, lemon-ginger tea warmed my hands. Howard asked what came next. I named jobs I could take, then said, lightly, “Maybe I’ll reconnect with Jonah.” His hand jerked; tea burned his skin. He waved it off, then admitted Jonah was struggling. “Have you talked to someone?” he asked me, voice careful. “About the nightmares?” I said they’d faded. They hadn’t. I woke with clammy hands, certain they were slick with blood. I’d started locking my door—to keep myself from the knives.

I showed him the award again. He said, “I can’t imagine,” flat as cooled tea. He’d changed. He rarely smiled. He chose his words like each one might set off a trap. When I stood to leave, he trailed me to the door and stumbled over our names. “Bye, Jonah—John. Sorry. You look so alike.” The air thinned. The kitchen too close. Knives glinting. His eyes were wide and white. Fear.

He had reason. I couldn’t kill my maker, not like Jonah killed his always-better-than-him clone, John. The night Jonah went to my house and lifted the knife, he told himself he was taking back what was his: his face, his future. He wears my life now—my name, my awards, my home key in his pocket. But the fit rubs. Howard knows or suspects. I watch it happen from inside the ruin he made of me, trapped behind my own memories while the world calls him John. I met my maker. He made me, and Jonah unmade me. Now the choice makes us both.
